description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

description: Symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured. Scenario: If the NTP server changes, a connection lost and connection established is logged to the event log. Workaround: The fix disables logging of normal server changes and only logs events if server reachability reaches 0 or NTP synchronization is lost.

description: Symptom: An STP port incorrectly transitions into Disabled/Blocked state. Scenario: During certain port flap events, STP may erroneously mark a port as Disabled/Blocked, even when the link should remain active. Workaround: Shut down all affected ports. Restart the hpe-pvstd process using the following commands: start-shell sudo bash ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep systemctl restart hpe-pvstd ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep exit exit

description: Symptom: Older log files are not removed during log rotation. Scenario: When the maximum log file size is reached, newly created logs fail to be added to the chunk files. As a result, older chunk log files become stale and are not deleted as expected during log rotation. Workaround: Manually remove the older chunk log files before the log file reaches its maximum size to prevent stale files from accumulating.

description: Symptom: Frequent log entries of "HTTP <MAC-ADDRESS> - Invalid packet length or headers" during normal HTTP traffic, even when no real packet corruption exists. There is no functional impact with this issue. Scenario: This issue occurs on legitimate TCP packets to port 80 with no payload (ACK, SYN/ACK, FIN, keep-alive, window updates). Previously these were funneled into an error path (or indistinguishable from malformed cases), producing repeated ERR logs under moderate web or update traffic. Workaround: Disable HTTP user-agent in device fingerprinting configuration.

description: Symptom: Excessive CRC/FCS errors causing complete loss of network connectivity for client devices in large deployments. Scenario: This issue occurs when upgrading to versions 10.13.1101 or 10.13.1110 on 6300 series switches, where affected devices are connected via cables longer than 60 meters to SmartRate ports operating at 100 Mbps Ethernet speed. Workaround: Upgrade to version 10.13.1140, 15.1060, or 16.1010. This reverts PHY firmware to 5.7.3 and resolves the issue.

description: Symptom: Traffic is dropped on the standby port 2/1/2 of the 6300 switch when running software version 10.16.1006. Scenario: This problem is observed in a VSF stack where the standby switch's designated port is not passing traffic. description: Symptom: Excessive CRC/FCS errors causing complete loss of network connectivity for client devices in large deployments. Scenario: This issue occurs when upgrading to versions 10.13.1101 or 10.13.1110 on 6300 series switches, where affected devices are connected via cables longer than 60 meters to SmartRate ports operating at 100 Mbps Ethernet speed. Workaround: Upgrade to version 10.13.1140, 15.1060, or 16.1010. This reverts PHY firmware to 5.7.3 and resolves the issue.
